title: Medical Expense Reimbursement Plan (MERP) for Small Business

requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must establish employer-funded medical reimbursement plan allowing tax-free reimbursement of employee medical expenses with employer deduction, typically for businesses with few employees or family businesses
criteria: Employer establishes written IRC §105 plan document defining eligible employees and reimbursable expenses
Typically used by small businesses, family businesses, or businesses with owner-only or owner-spouse arrangements
Plan reimburses qualified medical expenses under IRC §213(d) including insurance premiums, deductibles, copays, uncovered expenses
Employer funds plan and deducts reimbursements as ordinary business expense
Reimbursements tax-free to employees for qualifying medical expenses
Must comply with nondiscrimination rules if self-insured plan; discriminatory plans tax benefits to highly compensated
Cannot discriminate in eligibility or benefits in favor of highly compensated employees under §105(h) testing
Often combined with high-deductible health plan to reimburse amounts below deductible threshold

merp_plan_design.employer_funded: Employer pays reimbursements; employee salary reduction contributions not permitted for §105 plans
merp_plan_design.tax_free_benefit: Qualified medical expense reimbursements excluded from employee income and not subject to payroll taxes
merp_plan_design.employer_deduction: Employer deducts reimbursements as compensation and ordinary business expense
merp_plan_design.qualified_expenses: IRC §213(d) medical expenses including insurance premiums, medical, dental, vision, prescriptions
merp_plan_design.s_corp_2_percent: Plan cannot cover S Corporation 2%+ shareholders and family members; different rules apply
merp_plan_design.nondiscrimination_testing: Self-insured §105(h) plans must pass eligibility and benefits nondiscrimination testing

restrictions: S Corporation 2%+ shareholders cannot participate in §105 plan; premiums must be included in W-2 wages
Sole proprietors and partners cannot benefit from MERP; plan only covers employees, not self-employed owners
Discriminatory plans cause highly compensated employees to include discriminatory excess benefits in income
Plan must be employer-funded; cannot allow employee salary reduction elections (that's FSA)
Reimbursement-only plans cannot be combined with HSA contributions unless structured carefully
Administrative complexity requires written plan document, claims processing, and compliance testing
ACA market reforms may affect certain types of reimbursement arrangements and require coordination
State insurance department regulations may impact plan design and administration requirements
